questions.question: What is the time complexity of the Floyd-Warshall algorithm on a graph with V vertices?
options: O(V · E log V)
O(V² log V)
O(V³)
O(V⁴)
questions.explanation: Floyd-Warshall has three nested loops — one over each intermediate vertex k, one over each source i, and one over each destination j. Each iteration does constant work (a comparison and optional update), giving O(V³). This is independent of the number of edges.

questions.question: Johnson's algorithm uses a potential function h[v] derived from Bellman-Ford. What does the reweighting w'(u,v) = w(u,v) + h[u] − h[v] guarantee?
options: It makes all edge weights equal to zero
It ensures all reweighted edges are non-negative while preserving shortest paths
It doubles the original shortest path distances
It removes all negative edges by making them zero
questions.explanation: The potential function h satisfies h[v] ≤ h[u] + w(u,v) for all edges (from the Bellman-Ford distances), which means w'(u,v) = w(u,v) + h[u] − h[v] ≥ 0. Along any path the h-values telescope and cancel, so shortest paths are preserved. This lets Dijkstra run on the reweighted graph.

questions.question: On a dense graph with V=500 vertices and E ≈ V² edges (no negative weights), which algorithm is expected to be fastest for APSP?
options: Floyd-Warshall — O(V³) beats O(V·E log V) ≈ O(V³ log V)
Johnson's algorithm — O(V·E log V) is always faster than O(V³)
Repeated Dijkstra — it skips the Bellman-Ford step
They are all the same on dense graphs
questions.explanation: Floyd-Warshall is O(V³). Johnson's and repeated Dijkstra are O(V·E log V) = O(V³ log V) when E = V². The log V factor makes Floyd-Warshall the winner on dense graphs. Johnson's advantage appears on sparse graphs where E ≪ V².

questions.question: After running Floyd-Warshall, how do you detect which vertex pairs are affected by a negative cycle?
options: Check if dist[i][i] > 0 for any vertex i
Check if dist[i][j] was updated on the final iteration of k
Check if dist[i][i] < 0 — vertex i lies on or reaches a negative cycle; any pair (u,v) where dist[u][i] and dist[i][v] are finite is also affected
Negative cycles cannot be detected by Floyd-Warshall
questions.explanation: A negative cycle exists iff dist[i][i] < 0 for some i after Floyd-Warshall completes. Any pair (u,v) where u can reach i and i can reach v will have undefined shortest paths (−∞). You check dist[u][i] ≠ ∞ and dist[i][v] ≠ ∞ to find all affected pairs.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the Floyd-Warshall algorithm for all-pairs shortest paths?
options: O(VE)
O(V^2 log V)
questions.explanation: Floyd-Warshall uses three nested loops over all vertices (k, i, j), giving O(V^3). It works by considering each vertex as a potential intermediate node and checking if routing through it shortens any pair's distance.
